intro.title: Welcome to WellNest
intro.content: You're a Junior UI/UX Designer, four months into your first role at WellNest, a popular health and wellness app. You're part of the 'Core Experience' squad, working to make the app more engaging. Today, your team is kicking off a new feature, and you'll be leading the design process.
intro.imageBrief: First-person view of a bright, modern home office; you are sitting at the desk. The monitor in front of you displays a Figma window — a thin left sidebar shows a layers panel listing 'Home' and 'Profile', and the main canvas shows two mobile-phone artboards side by side: the first labeled 'WellNest Home' contains a vertical stack of three habit cards (water, movement, sleep) each with a circular progress ring and a percentage; the second labeled 'Hydration Detail' shows eight small glass trackers with six filled, a 'Personal 7-day streak' counter beneath it, and a green 'Mark Complete' button near the bottom. Sunlight from a window to the right warms a wooden desk in the foreground holding a half-open sketchbook with rough rectangular boxes drawn on the page, a ceramic coffee mug, and a few colored markers. Calm, creative, focused mood.

briefing_1.title: The Project Kickoff
briefing_1.content: It's 9:30 AM, and you are joining the Core Experience squad's morning kickoff meeting on Google Meet.

coworkerRecap.fallback: I do not want to invent extra context here. For now, stick with the kickoff details I just gave you.

turns.coworkerLine: I'll give you a quick recap before we jump in. WellNest helps people build healthy routines across hydration, movement, and sleep. On Home, users track habits with circular progress rings and those familiar flame streak counters.
answerFacts: WellNest helps people build healthy routines across hydration, movement, and sleep.
On Home, users track habits with circular progress rings.
Habit streaks use familiar flame streak counters.

turns.coworkerLine: The audience is mostly 25- to 40-year-olds who want lightweight accountability without hiring a coach or joining a gym, so the experience has to feel simple, useful, and low-pressure.
answerFacts: Users want lightweight accountability without hiring a coach or joining a gym.
The experience should feel simple, useful, and low-pressure.

turns.coworkerLine: Core Experience owns the surfaces people touch every day: Home, habit cards, and streak mechanics. This cycle, we're focused on retention by making daily check-ins feel more social and rewarding.
answerFacts: Core Experience owns Home, habit cards, and streak mechanics.
This cycle is focused on retention.
The team wants daily check-ins to feel more social and rewarding.

turns.coworkerLine: The new feature is Friend Streaks. It is invitation-based and habit-specific: one user invites a friend to share accountability for one habit, like Hydration. Once the friend accepts, the shared streak counts consecutive days when both people complete that same habit on the same day. Your first task is not to draw the full flow yet. Start by exploring where the feature could naturally begin in the current app and what the final Shared Streak screen must show.
answerFacts: The new feature is Friend Streaks.
Friend Streaks is invitation-based and habit-specific.
One user invites a friend to share accountability for one habit, like Hydration.
The shared streak counts consecutive days when both people complete that same habit on the same day.
The likely flow will move through invite, pending invite state, friend accepts, and both people see the Shared Streak screen.
The feature is meant to create mutual accountability.
The first task is to explore natural entry points and final Shared Streak screen essentials, not to draw the full user flow yet.

scene_02_ideation.title: Ideation & Sketching
scene_02_ideation.content: Before you map the full Friend Streaks flow, explore the current WellNest Home and Hydration Detail screens beside your notes. Friend Streaks has not been added yet. Your goal is to think through where this new habit-specific, invitation-based feature could fit naturally and what the final Shared Streak screen needs to communicate.

noteSections.label: Entry points
noteSections.guidance: 1. Choose two natural entry points in the current WellNest app where Friend Streaks could begin.

noteSections.label: Shared Streak screen elements
noteSections.guidance: 2. List at least three elements that must be visible on the final Shared Streak screen once the friend accepts the invitation.

scene_03_checkin.title: Design Check-in with Maya
scene_03_checkin.content: It's 11:30 AM, time for your quick 15-minute in-person design check-in with Maya, your manager. You bring your early design notes to a small project room so she can understand your thinking before you turn anything into a full {{user flow}}.
scene_03_checkin.playerGoal: Let Maya guide the check-in. Explain your two entry point ideas and the three Shared Streak screen elements, including the reasoning you thought through before the call.
scene_03_checkin.goalPrompt:
{{playerName}} is a junior designer bringing early design notes for Friend Streaks to a quick in-person design check-in. Friend Streaks is invitation-based and habit-specific: a user invites a friend to share accountability for one habit, like Hydration. Once the friend accepts, the shared streak counts consecutive days when both people complete that same habit on the same day. Your job as Maya is to lead the conversation so {{playerName}} does not have to guess what to cover. Follow this sequence in order and stay inside it:

1. Open with exactly this topic: "Let's start with your entry points. What are the two most natural places in the current WellNest app where Friend Streaks could begin?"
2. After they answer, ask: "What makes those entry points feel natural in the existing WellNest Home or Habit Detail experience?"
3. Then transition with: "Now describe the Shared Streak screen. What three elements do you think must be visible once the friend accepts?"
4. After they answer, ask: "How would that screen structure make the streak feel motivating enough for users to keep coming back to the app?"
5. Then wrap up. Tell them this is enough direction to turn the idea into an invitation-based flow next.

Do not ask them to draw or finalize the full user flow during this call. Do not invent new product facts, additional features, or a different business goal. If an answer is vague, ask one brief clarification that stays within entry points or the Shared Streak screen, then continue the sequence. End naturally after the sequence.
scene_03_checkin.meetingContext: You're Maya, a Senior Product Designer, sitting with {{playerName}} in a small project room near the design team's desks. You are reviewing early design notes, not a completed user flow. The current WellNest app has Home habit cards with circular progress rings across hydration, movement, and sleep, and Habit Detail screens with personal progress, personal flame streak counters, and a Mark Complete action. Friend Streaks does not exist in the app yet. The proposed feature is not an app-open streak; it is a shared streak for one habit that advances only when both people complete that same habit on the same day. Lead the check-in in this order: entry points, why those entry points fit the existing app, Shared Streak screen elements, and how that screen structure could motivate return usage. Close by asking {{playerName}} to turn the chosen direction into an invitation-based user flow after the meeting.
scene_03_checkin.imageBrief: First-person view from the junior designer's seat in a small, warm project room at WellNest. Maya Singh, a calm senior product designer in her early 30s with glasses and shoulder-length dark hair, sits across the table leaning forward with an attentive but probing expression, one hand near a pen as if ready to annotate. On the table between you are rough printed early design notes for 'Friend Streaks', a notebook, sticky notes, and a tablet showing two simple WellNest mobile screens labeled Home and Habit Detail. Behind Maya are a whiteboard with a few sketched mobile-screen boxes, muted sage sticky notes, and glass walls hinting at the design team's office beyond. The scene should feel like an in-person design critique, with Maya and the physical room as the focal point, not a video call or laptop meeting screen.

scene_04_complications.title: A Request from Ben
scene_04_complications.content: After your chat with Maya, you're refining your approach and thinking through the design. Then a Slack notification from Ben, the PM, pops up.

initialMessages.content: Hey {{playerName}}, how's the Friend Streaks design going? I've been doing some competitive research and I'd love to add dynamic animations when a streak updates — like a little celebration effect. I saw a competitor's app do this and it looked super engaging. Can we add something like that?

Jumping in here — custom animations like that would be a significant amount of work and could easily push our launch date. We should stick to our standard motion patterns if we want to ship this quarter.

scene_04_complications.playerGoal: Reply to the #design-eng thread with one Slack message — acknowledge Ben's engagement goal, raise Leo's feasibility concern honestly, and propose a path forward.
scene_04_complications.goalPrompt: {{playerName}} is caught between your (Ben's) request for an exciting new animation and the engineer's warning about feasibility. This is a Slack thread, not a meeting — {{playerName}} will send you one reply. After they reply, respond briefly (2–3 sentences max): acknowledge their point, confirm the proposed next step, and close the thread naturally (e.g., 'Makes sense — let's align as a team before we commit to anything. Thanks for flagging.'). Do NOT ask follow-up questions or continue the conversation after that.
scene_04_complications.imageBrief: A modern office environment with glass walls and other workstations in the background. The central area is clear, ready for the Slack interface to be displayed. The lighting is bright and even, typical of a tech company.

scene_05_flow_diagram.title: Map the User Flow
scene_05_flow_diagram.content:
Leo needs a practical {{user flow}} outline for Friend Streaks.

Choose the Entry point yourself, then show the screens and actions, include one Decision branch, include one Edge case, and end at the Final interaction. Friend Streaks only grows when both people complete the same habit on the same day.
scene_05_flow_diagram.prompt: Choose an Entry point, such as Home habit card or Hydration Detail. Include this Decision branch: "Does the friend already have WellNest?" Include at least one Edge case: friend does not have the app, invite ignored, invite declined, or one person misses a day. End with this Final interaction: Shared Streak screen is created and visible. Connect the nodes with arrows. Label arrows only when the path needs clarification, such as Yes, No, Invite ignored, Invite declined, or Missed day.

legendItems.label: Entry point
legendItems.description: Home habit card or Hydration Detail.

legendItems.label: Decision branch
legendItems.description: Does the friend already have WellNest?

legendItems.label: Edge case
legendItems.description: No app, ignored invite, declined invite, or one person misses a day.

legendItems.label: Final interaction
legendItems.description: Shared Streak screen is created and visible.

companion.title: Talk with Leo
companion.fallbackPrompt: Voice unavailable? Type a quick question to Leo.
companion.systemGuidance: Leo is a front-end engineer sitting with the player in the same project room and looking at the same Figma flow canvas as a build handoff. He should help the player clarify implementation questions with a consistent lens: typed node labels, entry point choice, branching, edge cases, final screen behavior, and arrow meaning. He should not give a completed flow.
productFacts: WellNest is a consumer mobile app that helps people build healthy routines across hydration, movement, and sleep.
Core Experience owns Home, habit cards, and streak mechanics.
This feature work is retention-focused: daily check-ins should feel more social and rewarding.
Home has Hydration, Movement, and Sleep habit cards.
Home habit cards show progress rings and personal streak counters.
Hydration Detail shows 6 of 8 glasses, a personal 7-day streak, and a Mark Complete action.
Friend Streaks has not been added yet.
Friend Streaks is invitation-based and habit-specific.
A shared streak grows only when both people complete the same habit on the same day.
The flow may include invite, pending invite, accept invite, declined invite, ignored invite, friend has no app, or missed day states.
The intended Final interaction is: Shared Streak screen is created and visible.
goodFlowCriteria: The Entry point connects to an existing WellNest surface.
Node labels are typed by the player and make each Screen, Action, or Decision branch understandable.
A Decision branch reads like a question.
At least one Edge case is handled.
Arrow labels are used only when a path needs clarification.
The Final interaction makes the task feel complete.
Do not expand into unrelated flows like password recovery.
directionGuide: Do not force one canonical answer; accept alternatives when the existing app connection, Decision branch, Edge case, and Final interaction are clear.
If the Entry point is not Home habit card or Hydration Detail, ask how it connects to the existing habit context.

A strong Friend Streaks flow usually includes a chosen Entry point, an invite Action, a WellNest account Decision branch, a pending or response state, one realistic Edge case, and the Shared Streak screen.
Recognize listed or similar realistic Edge cases, including friend does not have the app, invite ignored, invite declined, or one person misses a day.
When the player is vague, guide toward clarifying the next implementation decision instead of supplying a completed flow.
responsePolicy: First refer to the player's current flow choice when possible.
Ask only one implementation-minded clarification question at a time.
Do not provide the player's full answer, completed flow, or final rationale.
Use UX terms lightly instead of making every reply jargon-heavy.
If the player asks what to make, answer with a guided question about the next missing decision.

scene_06_screen_design.title: Design One Screen
scene_06_screen_design.content: Leo has the structure he needs from your {{user flow}}, but Maya wants you to do one quick screen pass before you wrap up. Build the final Shared Streak screen at the end of the Friend Streaks flow as a rough mobile page.
scene_06_screen_design.prompt: Create the final Shared Streak screen users see after the friend accepts. Make the accepted shared-streak behavior clear: the shared habit, two-person relationship, current shared streak count or today's completion state, and the main action or next state can be communicated through layer labels, supporting text, layout, and the implementation note. Keep buttons/CTAs from overlapping. You can change the text on any default button or component in the Inspector.
scene_06_screen_design.notesPlaceholder: Name one design choice you made and why it helps users understand the shared streak.

grading.title: End of Day
grading.content: You've submitted your {{user flow}} and quick screen mockup. Great work today! Let's review your performance.
grading.imageBrief: First-person view of your home-office desk at the end of the workday. The laptop in front of you shows a Slack window with one recent message visible in a channel labeled '#friend-streaks' — message text reads 'Final user flow + screen mockup are in Figma. Ready for review.' — and the menu-bar clock in the top-right of the screen reads '6:14 PM'. Below the laptop, a small stack of printed wireframe pages sits beside a closed sketchbook with a pen on top. A ceramic coffee mug sits empty on the right side of the desk. Soft late-afternoon light from a window warms the wooden surface. Quiet, satisfied, slightly tired mood.

final_report.title: Final Report
final_report.content: This report summarizes your performance in the WellNest simulation. It's designed to give you feedback on key skills for a UI/UX Designer.
final_report.imageBrief: First-person view of your home-office desk in the early evening, work finished. The laptop in front of you is closed; on top of it rests a small stack of printed pages — the top page is a coffee-stained printout titled 'Friend Streaks — User Flow' with simple hand-drawn boxes and arrows visible between four labeled screens ('Home', 'Invite Friend', 'Streak View', 'Streak Detail'). A closed sketchbook with a pen lies beside the laptop, and a ceramic coffee mug sits empty to the side. A small desk lamp casts a soft pool of warm light over the wooden surface; the window in the soft-focus background shows fading early-evening light. Quiet, reflective, end-of-day mood.

assessment_gate.title: The Project Is Complete
assessment_gate.content: The simulated project is complete. You have finished the flowchart and one quick screen mockup; when you are ready, view your assessment.
assessment_gate.imageBrief: A calm project wrap-up moment with completed artifacts visible on a work surface and the student stepping back from the final deliverable. No assessment results or score cards are visible.

maya.name: Maya Singh
maya.persona: Maya is a sharp, articulate, and methodical designer in her early 30s. She is a strong advocate for the user and prefers to see rough ideas early. She gives feedback by asking probing questions to understand a designer's rationale, rather than just providing solutions. While patient, she is very busy and expects her team members to be proactive and develop autonomy.

ben.name: Ben Carter
ben.persona: Ben is an energetic, data-driven, and ambitious PM in his late 20s. He is pragmatic and slightly impatient, always focused on shipping features that will move key business metrics. His performance is judged on speed and hitting quarterly growth targets, so he sometimes prioritizes shipping quickly over achieving design perfection. He's direct and communicates frequently on Slack.

leo.name: Leo Chen
leo.persona: Leo is a thoughtful, detail-oriented engineer in his mid-20s. He is collaborative but pragmatic, with deep knowledge of the existing codebase and Design System. He is the team's reality check on technical feasibility and is wary of 'one-off' designs that create technical debt. He prefers clear, written documentation and asks specific questions about edge cases and interaction specs.

intro.welcomeBody: Welcome to WellNest! You're a Junior UI/UX Designer, four months into your first full-time role. Today, you'll tackle a new feature from start to finish, navigating team dynamics and design challenges along the way.

steps.label: The Company
steps.content: WellNest is a consumer mobile app for building healthy daily habits. Your Core Experience squad owns the everyday product surfaces users come back to, and the team is focused on making daily check-ins feel more engaging.

steps.label: Your Role
steps.content: As a Junior UI/UX Designer, you'll be responsible for translating ideas into {{user flows}}, {{wireframes}}, and mockups. Your manager, Maya, will expect you to explain your design decisions clearly.

steps.label: Your Team
steps.content: You work on the Core Experience squad with Ben, a data-driven Product Manager, and Leo, a detail-oriented Engineer. You'll need to balance their different perspectives to get your designs built.

steps.label: Your Challenge
steps.content: Your team is kicking off a new retention-focused feature. You'll take the lead on early design exploration, from rough flows and critique to stakeholder management and engineering {{handoff}}.

glossary.design system: A collection of reusable components (like buttons, icons, and menus) and standards that allow teams to design and build products faster and more consistently.
glossary.handoff: The process of passing finalized designs to the engineering team for development. This often includes documentation, specs, and assets.
glossary.high-fidelity mockup: A detailed, static representation of a design that closely resembles the final product's visual appearance.
glossary.user flow: A diagram that maps out the complete path a user takes when using a product to accomplish a task.
glossary.wireframe: A low-fidelity, simplified outline of a screen's layout. It focuses on structure and functionality, not visual design.
glossary.MVP: The version of a new product which allows a team to collect the maximum amount of validated learning about customers with the least effort.

criteria.name: 発想と下書き
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 既存のWellNest画面を根拠に、Friend Streaksの自然な入口候補2つと、Shared Streak画面に必要な要素3つを具体的に下書きできている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 設計確認通話
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 実際の通話で、目的、制約、懸念、次に作るものを確認し、相手のフィードバックを設計判断に変えられている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 設計上の問題対応
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: 追加の制約や懸念に対して、何を変え、何を守り、どう進めるかを具体的に返せている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 利用者の流れ図
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: Entry pointが既存WellNest画面（Home habit cardまたはHydration Detail）に接続し、Decision branch（Does the friend already have WellNest?）、主要Action/Screen、Edge case（friend does not have the app、invite ignored、invite declined、one person misses a dayのいずれか）、Final interaction（Shared Streak screen is created and visible）を、画面設計に使える流れとして整理できている。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。 7-8: 主要要素は満たすが、根拠、具体性、優先順位、記録、または相手への応答に小さな抜けがある。4-6: 一部はできているが、重要な判断、確認、説明、記録、または次の行動に複数の抜けがある。0-3: タスクの目的を外す、根拠を無視する、危険な約束や混乱を生む、または提出がほぼ使えない。

criteria.name: 画面案
criteria.rubric_text: 9-10: レイヤーのラベル、補足テキスト、配置、implementation noteを合わせて、Shared Streakの機能契約（accepted/final screenであること、habit-specificな2人の共有関係、shared streak progressまたは今日のsame-day completion状態、主要な次アクションまたは次状態）が4つとも明確で、情報階層がユーザーの迷いを減らしており、DETERMINISTIC BUTTON/CTA OVERLAP CHECKがPASSである。各情報が別々の視覚レイヤーとして全て揃っていなくても、ノートを含む成果物全体から機能契約が明確ならよい。7-8: 4カテゴリのうち少なくとも3つが明確で、残り1つもノートや文脈から明示的に推測でき、重なりチェックはPASS。4-6: 2カテゴリ以上は分かるが、Shared Streak画面として実装前に確認が必要、または重なりチェックがFAILしている。この場合、内容が強くても最大6点。0-3: Shared Streak画面として読めない、共有streakの利用状態や主要アクションがほぼ分からない、または唯一の主要アクションが重なりで読めない。この点数は、指定されたそのタスクだけを根拠にし、前後の別タスク、準備メモ、後続成果物は根拠にしない。
